mkt-cold-email.title: Cold email to a Head of Data
mkt-cold-email.contextCopy: You're drafting a cold email to a Head of Data at a mid-sized insurance company. Goal: get a 20-minute intro call about your new LLM governance product.
mkt-cold-email.prompt: Write a 90-word cold email to Priya, Head of Data at NorthStar Insurance. I sell an LLM proxy that gives admins budget control and audit trails across OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google. Opening line should reference the real-world pain of AI bills arriving unexpectedly. Close with a specific ask for 20 mins next week.
mkt-cold-email.teachingNote: Gemini 2.5 Flash often costs 1/10th what GPT-4o does on short-form marketing copy — and on tasks like this, the gap in quality is narrower than the gap in price.

Three Heads of Data I've spoken with this quarter all opened the same way:
an AI bill came in 2-4× their forecast,
and nobody in the room could attribute it to a team or model.
Allotly is an OpenAI-compatible proxy in front of OpenAI,
Admins get per-team budgets with hard cutoffs (not soft alerts),
per-call audit logs that satisfy SOC 2 and internal review,
and model allowlists enforced server-side rather than in app code.
the claims-summarization workflow is where this usually pays back inside a quarter.

The pattern I keep seeing at insurers your size is this:
a workflow starts as an experiment,
and you find out it has graduated when the LLM invoice arrives at three times the forecast —
with no per-team breakdown to defend it.
Allotly sits in front of OpenAI,
and Google as a single OpenAI-compatible endpoint.
Admins set per-team budgets that genuinely halt spend rather than send an apologetic Slack message after the fact.
Every call is logged in a form your auditors will accept,
and your model allowlist is enforced at the proxy rather than in twelve different application repos.
I think NorthStar is one quarter away from needing exactly this layer.
Could I take 20 minutes next Wednesday to show you how it would slot in?

mkt-linkedin-post.title: LinkedIn thought-leadership post
mkt-linkedin-post.contextCopy: You're posting on LinkedIn as a founder. Goal: frame LLM governance as the next inevitable layer of enterprise software, and hint at what you're building without over-selling.
mkt-linkedin-post.prompt: Write a 150-word LinkedIn post arguing that LLM governance (budgets, audit, multi-provider routing) is the next SSO — invisible infrastructure that becomes non-negotiable once the CFO notices. No emojis. No bullet points. One vivid line early, a clear thesis, one specific example.
mkt-linkedin-post.teachingNote: Short-form thought leadership is where Claude's prose quality shines — but you're paying 10-20x for that polish.

res-summarize.title: Summarize a methodology paragraph
res-summarize.contextCopy: You're a PhD student reviewing a dense methodology paragraph from a published paper on differential privacy in federated learning. You need a plain-English summary for your supervisor.
res-summarize.prompt: Summarize this in 3 sentences for a non-specialist: 'We employ a local differential privacy mechanism with ε-budget of 0.5, applied via Gaussian noise injection on per-client gradients before secure aggregation. Composition is tracked using the Rényi accountant; clients exceeding their per-round budget are dropped. Utility is preserved through calibrated clipping thresholds derived from the 90th percentile of per-layer gradient norms across the warmup cohort.'
res-summarize.teachingNote: Research tasks are where reasoning quality shows. Watch which model translates jargon faithfully vs. which glosses it.

The system protects each client's contributions by adding carefully calibrated noise to their model updates before anything is shared,
with the strength of that protection capped by a fixed privacy budget.
A specialized accountant tracks how much of that budget each round consumes,
and any client who would exceed their share is excluded for that round.
To keep the model accurate despite the noise,
the size of each update is trimmed using thresholds learned from a warm-up group rather than chosen by hand.
Each participant's contribution to the shared model is deliberately blurred with mathematical noise before anyone outside their device can see it,
with an overall ceiling on how much privacy can be spent across the system.
A precise accountant keeps a running tally of that spending and excludes any participant in danger of overshooting it on a given round.
To make sure the noise does not cripple accuracy,
the system caps how large each update can be using thresholds it learned automatically from an initial warm-up group,
rather than relying on hand-tuned numbers.

res-critique.title: Find the logical gap in this argument
res-critique.contextCopy: You're peer-reviewing a blog post about AI safety. Find the weakest logical step in the argument and explain it simply.
res-critique.prompt: Identify the single weakest step in this argument: 'Large language models hallucinate. Therefore they cannot be trusted in medical contexts. Therefore they should not be deployed in hospitals. Therefore any hospital using them is acting recklessly.' Be specific. No hedging.
res-critique.teachingNote: On explicit reasoning tasks, frontier models pull ahead. Compare how each model names the fallacy.

chunks.delta: The weakest step is the jump
The first claim is about absolute reliability;
the second treats deployment as binary.
In practice, no medical tool is fully trusted —
all fail sometimes and are deployed anyway,
can be scoped, supervised, or advisory rather than autonomous.
to "should not be deployed in hospitals."
It equates being untrustworthy as an oracle
with being useless as a tool.
Hospitals already deploy many unreliable tools —
Medicine is full of unreliable tools —
The argument ignores that third option entirely.
The weakest step is the move
This conflates two very different claims:
and being useless as a tool under supervision.
Hospitals already deploy unreliable tools constantly —
with verification, scope limits, and second opinions.
The argument simply ignores the supervised-deployment option.
under oversight, with scope limits and verification.
The argument skips that third option
and jumps straight to a categorical ban.
use it autonomously or not at all.
Hospitals deploy unreliable tools all the time
The argument skips the supervised-use option entirely.
The weakest step is the second:
"Therefore they cannot be trusted in medical contexts."
"Hallucinate" is a property of unconstrained generation.
"Cannot be trusted" is a property of a deployed system,
which includes the model plus its retrieval layer,
and the human in the loop.
The argument silently equates a raw capability of the model with a property of every conceivable deployment of it —
and that equivocation is what carries every downstream conclusion.
Once that equivalence breaks (a model used only to draft notes a clinician must approve,
The weakest step is the second:
"Therefore they cannot be trusted in medical contexts."
It conflates a property of the model in isolation —
that it sometimes produces false statements —
with a property of the deployed system,
which is the model plus retrieval,
The argument quietly assumes those layers either do not exist or cannot meaningfully reduce harm,
and from that unstated assumption every later step inherits its force.
Strip out the equivocation and the chain collapses:
a model that hallucinates inside a workflow designed to catch hallucinations is not the same artifact as a model trusted to act unsupervised.

cre-tweet.title: 280-character pitch
cre-tweet.contextCopy: You're launching a product on X. You need one tweet that gets people to click through.
cre-tweet.prompt: Write a single tweet (280 chars max) for Allotly: an LLM proxy that lets admins allocate budgets across OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google, with hard enforcement and audit logs. Tone: confident, slightly wry, no jargon, no emojis, no hashtags. One sentence is fine.
cre-tweet.teachingNote: Constrained creative tasks are where quality differences are smallest and cost differences biggest.

cre-explain-5.title: Explain it like I'm five
cre-explain-5.contextCopy: A kid asked you what Allotly does. Explain it in a way that makes sense to them — and, quietly, to their parents too.
cre-explain-5.prompt: Explain Allotly to a 5-year-old. Use one simple metaphor (candy, allowance, pocket money — anything kid-friendly). 80 words max. Keep it warm.
cre-explain-5.teachingNote: Creative mode is where outputs diverge most — and where 'best' becomes taste.

Imagine you and your friends share a big jar of candy at school,
and three different stores keep dropping more candy in.
It's hard to tell who took what,
or when the jar will be empty.
Allotly is like a kind helper who stands next to the jar.
They give every kid their own little cup,
write down what each one takes,
and gently say "all done!" when a cup is empty —
so nobody ends up with a tummy ache and the grown-ups stay happy.
Imagine your whole class shares one big piggy bank to buy snacks from three different shops,
and nobody knows who's spending what.
Sometimes a kid buys way too much and the piggy bank is empty before lunch.
Allotly is like a friendly grown-up who stands next to the piggy bank.
Every kid gets their own little allowance jar,
the grown-up writes down each snack,
and gently says "all done!" when the jar is empty —
so the snacks are fair and nobody runs out.
